ground_truth.title: What legal options do I have against a moving company (based in FL, storing our home goods in CA, we're in NYC) who has
ground_truth.main_content: Customer asks about legal options against a moving company based in Florida that is storing their home goods in California while they are in New York. The company has breached its contract by neglecting to move goods within the contracted 30 business days. Attorney Ashley Paige Esq provides legal advice explaining that the moving company would be in breach of contract, allowing the customer to sue for monetary damages and/or specific performance. Before suing, a demand letter should be served identifying the breach and demanding transport within a specific timeframe. If alternate movers had to be hired at higher cost, the customer could sue for the amount paid plus the difference. Additional expenses like hotel costs for delayed belongings could also be included. The broker who selected the movers could also potentially be liable. While a DOT claim could be filed, it is not required before pursuing legal action. Small claims court in the state where the movers are located would be appropriate, and hiring an attorney is advisable given the out-of-state location.
with: The moving company would be in breach of the contract so you could sue for breach and request monetary damage and/or specific performance.
If you hired alternate movers that were more expensive you could sue the original company for the amount you paid them plus the difference with the new movers.
Generally, before suing on this type of claim you would serve a demand letter.
Yes given you are out of state, hiring an attorney would be best.
You are not required to file with the DOT.
